Data.MeaningHint: Picture this child scooting to his fancy, expensive university in a little suit. Compared to the other kids, his wheels are quite humble!
Data.ReadingHint: You even bought your son this humble scooter and you really hope they don't grow up to be a selfish, spoiled kid this way.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>humble</kanji> child is your <reading>son</reading> (そん). You don't want any child of yours flaunting his wealth and good standing in the faces of other people. You've taught them to be humble.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: On this <radical>scooter</radical> is a <radical>child</radical> who's trying to show that his <radical>lineage</radical> is <kanji>humble</kanji>. All of the other children are going around in fancy cars or helicopters, but this one child wants to be a humble child of the people, so he scoots on a plebeian scooter.

Data.MeaningHint: Just make sure you don't inquire about anything inappropriate, or you'll end up facing an inquiry from HR…
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine saying "Ooh, Kaga!" to this overly curious leader-director-chef. Say it out loud, like he's just inquired about something weird. He just really loves cooking, okay? But he inquires too much about it in the workplace, so you have to let him know how you feel.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Turns out the leader and director of your company is Chairman Kaga from the TV show Iron Chef. Every time he tries to <kanji>inquire</kanji> about weird stuff, you have to say <reading>Ooh, Kaga</reading> (うかが) disapprovingly. "What are you doing for dinner tonight?" he inquires. Ooh, Kaga! "What's the best way to soften an eggplant?" he inquires. Ooh, Kaga!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Being a <radical>leader</radical> or <radical>director</radical> in a company means you have to <kanji>inquire</kanji> about lots of things. Have you started this yet? Have you finished that yet? Did you get that thing I sent you? Where's my coffee? Basically if you're a leader or director, your main job is to inquire about these very important things.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a single beam of moonlight penetrating through the winter sky, right onto the trash.
Data.ReadingHint: The clear, penetrating beam tastes exactly like iron, which is something you really weren't expecting. But now it's filling your entire mouth and you don't want it to stop.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You want the light to <kanji>penetrate</kanji> you, so you put your face in the way of the beam and it shoots <kanji>clear</kanji> through you. Your mouth fills with the taste of <reading>鉄</reading> (てつ).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You decide to <radical>loiter</radical> next to the <radical>trash</radical> under the <radical>moon</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> so the moon's light will <kanji>penetrate</kanji> you. The sky is <kanji>clear</kanji>est in winter, and this spot next to the trash happens to get the most of it.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture the bundle of geoducks getting smashed by the tsunami, breaking apart, and getting tossed around in the rapids.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine the cool shallow water lapping at your feet as you bite into some crunchy celery and watch the rapids in the distance.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: While those geoducks are getting tossed about in the <kanji>rapids</kanji>, you're sitting in the <kanji>shallows</kanji> chomping down on some <reading>ce</reading>lery (せ).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits a <radical>bundle</radical> of <radical>geoduck</radical>s, swirling around their weird shapes, and creating <kanji>rapids</kanji>! The rapids are so violent, some of the geoducks break out of the bundle and get washed into the <kanji>shallows</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine you need proof of your withdrawal so badly, that you're in the trash like this, raking around with your fingers in the moonlight. Close your eyes and feel the cold, smell the stink.
Data.ReadingHint: The pain from the 鉄 reverberates up your fingers and into your hands and arms. What's this great lump of 鉄 doing in the trash?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: While looking for the <kanji>withdrawal</kanji> letter, your fingers hit a piece of <reading>鉄</reading> (てつ). Ouch!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You use your <radical>fingers</radical> to dig through the <radical>trash</radical> in the light of the <radical>moon</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> because somewhere in there is your <kanji>withdrawal</kanji> letter. What did you withdraw? Your resignation? Your troops? Whatever it was, you need to find the proof, and fast.

Data.MeaningHint: What would have to happen for you to set aside your own fingers? Try to imagine it right now.
Data.ReadingHint: Think of all the things you do with your fingers. Now picture how they would look if you had never ever cleaned them with soap. Uhg, disgusting. You should just set them aside here, and find some new ones.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: I know why they <kanji>set aside</kanji> their fingers long ago, it's because there was no <reading>so</reading>ap (そ). If you didn't have soap to clean your fingers you wouldn't want to keep them for very long...
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You find some <radical>fingers</radical> from <radical>long ago</radical>. They must have been <kanji>set aside</kanji> by their previous owners. They definitely weren't cut off or anything, someone just decided they didn't want them anymore and set them aside.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine tracing your fingers over the table, the cold winter frost stinging them, as you reveal the secrets of this table and what it was based on.
Data.ReadingHint: The buildings and temples are all very childlike. Like crayon drawn on a table.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This particular table was <kanji>based on</kanji> Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). Little buildings and temples, that sort of look like Little Kyoto. Problem is, it was made by a <reading>子</reading> (こ), so it's not very good.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Use your <radical>fingers</radical> to wipe this <radical>winter</radical> <radical>table</radical> and see what it was <kanji>based on</kanji>. The cold winter weather covers the table in frost, and when you wipe it away, you can see traces of what the table was originally based on. All tables have to be based on something, even if it's just other tables that came before them.

Data.MeaningHint: Have him run your ceremony for you. Think about why he is so righteous, then think about an extremely righteous ceremony.
Data.ReadingHint: *CHOP*
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Sadly, the reason only the righteous can run this <kanji>ceremony</kanji> is because it's a punishment ceremony, where he pulls the cord to cut someone's head off via a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of <radical>righteousness</radical> is the only one who can run this <kanji>ceremony</kanji>. Why? Because it's a very pure ceremony, and only those with an extremely righteous mind can run it.

Data.MeaningHint: There are a lot of hoodlums out there that try to dress as trees and confuse people. So make sure you measure their drum before you cut one down or tell someone your secrets under it.
Data.ReadingHint: However! A non-wood tree will be really thirsty from pretending to be a wood tree. It will start making sounds and moving and probably say, "Can I have a juice box, please?"
Data.ReadingMnemonic: If you're still not sure if the tree is really made of <kanji>wood</kanji>, just take out a small <reading>ju</reading>ice (じゅ) box and start drinking it. A wooden tree will show no interest in the juice box at all.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Each <radical>tree</radical> needs its <radical>drum</radical> <radical>measurement</radical> done before you can say it's really made of <kanji>wood</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Most people abandon fruit when it's misshapen. It's probably fine though...
Data.ReadingHint: You abandon the fruit and stare intently at this key. It must be why the fruit was weird looking and abandoned in the trash... but what does it go to?! You dig through the trash and try the key on everything that's been abandoned in there.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You reach into the trash, take out the <kanji>abandon</kanji>ed fruit, and bite into it. OUCH! There's a <reading>key</reading> (き) inside, and you just bit into it.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>trash</radical> is a weird looking <radical>fruit</radical>. Someone decided to <kanji>abandon</kanji> it here.

Data.MeaningHint: Look at the tiger running with his tiger legs. So majestic. So fierce.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine watching the tiger as it notices the Torah and then runs away. The tiger sounds as if it is screaming in terror as it flees from the proximity of the Torah.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>tiger</kanji> is using his legs to run away from the <reading>Torah</reading> (とら). This tiger is deathly afraid of religion, so a holy book like the Torah causes the tiger to run away.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tiger</radical> has <radical>legs</radical> that belong to a <kanji>tiger</kanji>. You know, a tiger has the legs of a tiger. This is common knowledge. Nothing new here.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine this little firefly blinking his firefly light as he flies around shouting a Viking warcry and brandishing an axe. Yaaaargh!
Data.ReadingHint: Hear Hotaru’s shrill scream as she runs away from the Viking firefly. If you don’t know what Hotaru Tomoe looks like, Google a picture of her to get a better visualization.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The Viking <kanji>firefly</kanji> is attacking <reading>Hotaru</reading> (ほたる) Tomoe (alter ego of Sailor Saturn) because the Viking firefly hates the show Sailor Moon and everyone on it. Hotaru screams in terror as she is deathly afraid of fireflies, especially Viking ones.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>Viking</radical> <radical>insect</radical> is a <kanji>firefly</kanji>. In the insect kingdom, the insect most resembling a Viking is undoubtedly the firefly.

Data.MeaningHint: This particular insect signpost points you to where the bee is.
Data.ReadingHint: You got to see a single bee hatch! What a beautiful sight. Weird that a signpost points to it.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Thanks to the insect signpost you find the <kanji>bee</kanji>. You make it just in time to see the bee <reading>hatch</reading> (はち) from its egg.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: On the <radical>insect</radical> <radical>signpost</radical> there is a single sign that says <kanji>bee</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the hard liquor as you measure it out. Hear it pouring out of the bottle into a little measuring cup as you catch the whiff of strong alcohol in the air.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine Chewbacca drinking hard liquor all the time while Han Solo told him he needed to stop and get help, but Chewbacca just did his yell thing and drank more hard liquor.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You know who enjoyed <kanji>hard liquor</kanji> a lot? <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう). He was always drinking, which is concerning when you think about all the driving he did.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>alcohol</radical> that needs <radical>measurement</radical> is <kanji>hard liquor</kanji>. Drinking too much hard liquor isn't good for you, so you must make sure to measure it out in precise quantities.

Data.MeaningHint: Bees are a certain insect that build a roof around their honey.
Data.ReadingHint: Your thick mitts make it so the bees can't even sting your hands. Unfortunately, you are naked and only wearing mitts, what were you thinking?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: To get the <kanji>honey</kanji> out, you need to put on special <reading>mitts</reading> (みつ) so your hands don't get stung.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical> (hive) on a <radical>certain</radical> <radical>insect</radical> (a bee) is what protects all of the <kanji>honey</kanji> or <kanji>nectar</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine looking through these ruins. Picture the broken tiger and cactus statues. Try to imagine the people who used to live here before it turned to ruins.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the ruins of Little Kyoto. Try to imagine what it was like before the whole place fell apart. Maybe they would have lived on if they hadn't been doing things with sharp cacti and bitey tigers.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Well, as it turns out, these are the <kanji>ruins</kanji> of Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ), the city that existed before the modern day one. And it looks like the citizens of Little Kyoto faced their end a long time ago.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You're digging in the <radical>dirt</radical> when you find a <radical>tiger</radical> and a <radical>cactus</radical>. You keep digging until you realize that these are a part of some big <kanji>ruins</kanji>. They're the ruins of tiger and cactus statues. It looks like whoever used to live here worshiped tigers and cacti. Or maybe they ate them. But now their whole civilization is just ruins.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a huge warship overseeing everything around it. It's big and kinda scary.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture Genghis Khan aboard a monstrous warship. He's carefully watching the sea and land that he's conquered.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: This huge <kanji>warship</kanji> is run by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん), the famous warlord. Really, what did you expect? Who else is strong enough to captain a warship but him?
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>boat</radical> that <radical>oversee</radical>s other boats, land, and people is usually a <kanji>warship</kanji>. They're the big boats that oversee everything around them. And if anyone steps out of line, a warship can take care of it.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine witnessing this tsunami taking the two husbands up into the sun. Hear the water rushing. That tsunami is massive. What are they going to conceal in the sun?
Data.ReadingHint: Just make sure to remember the husbands are riding that tsunami alongside their centaur, heading towards the sun. They need to conceal the centaur to keep it safe.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The husbands are going to <kanji>conceal</kanji> their pet <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) in the sun. The local government isn't too pleased that they have a pet centaur, so they have to conceal it in the sun so it doesn't get confiscated.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> carries two <radical>husband</radical>s up into the <radical>sun</radical>, where they're going to <kanji>conceal</kanji> something.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture the gladiator squeezing his hand into a tight fist, raising it up and yelling.
Data.ReadingHint: Ken and the gladiator clash! Fists fly! You've never seen hand-to-hand combat like this! You can hardly keep up!!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The gladiator's <kanji>fist</kanji> is now aimed at <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai. Little does the gladiator know, Ken knows the ways of the samurai and the gladiator. He slowly raises his own hands and turns them into fists.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>gladiator</radical> turns his <radical>hand</radical> into a <kanji>fist</kanji>. A fist for punching other gladiators.

Data.MeaningHint: You’re angry that you could only find charcoal so you take the charcoal in your hands and you crush it atop the cliff on the mountain. Feel the charcoal breaking in your hands as the wind carries the charcoal dust off from the top of the cliff on the mountain.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine lying out in the sun next to the crushed charcoal, thinking you’re going to get tan. You’re very surprised when you actually catch on fire. Hot hot hot!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: After crushing the <kanji>charcoal</kanji>, you decide to take advantage of your current location and lie out in the sun for a while to get a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). Instead of getting a tan though, you catch on fire. Oops.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You climb up a <radical>mountain</radical> and a <radical>cliff</radical> in search of <radical>fire</radical>, but all you find is <kanji>charcoal</kanji>. You can make fire with the charcoal, but you were hoping to find fire itself. All that climbing for nothing.

Data.MeaningHint: Look at this fire burning the rice paddy and the surrounding field. Smell burnt rice in the air as you see the flames dance before your eyes.
Data.ReadingHint:
Imagine running around this hot cay as you try to put out the fire in your field. The fire is ravaging the entire hot cay, so it takes you a while to put it out. Man those flames are hot.

Also, this kanji can have the reading はた for the kun'yomi as well. If you learn the longer version (はたけ) it will be easier to just shave off a kana to remember the shorter version.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Your rice paddy farm and <kanji>field</kanji> are on a cay. A cay is a small, sandy island, and since your farm is on fire, this cay has turned into a hot cay. Or as Mario would say, that’s one-a <reading>hot-a cay</reading>! (はたけ) Mama mia!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>fire</radical> in the <radical>rice paddy</radical> burns down the <kanji>field</kanji>. The rice paddy fire spreads out from the paddy and consumes the field next to it. This rice paddy fire has ravaged your entire farm!

Data.MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
Data.ReadingHint: Home is where the wrapped heart is.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Think about being <kanji>wrap</kanji>ped up, nice and warm and safe in your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). Home is where you wrap yourself in a blanket, in the warmth of the fireplace, in the arms of those you love.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>wrap</radical> radical and the <kanji>wrap</kanji> kanji are the same!

Data.MeaningHint: Reflect upon your own humanity. What does it mean to be human? Is it wearing shoes and hats? Probably.
Data.ReadingHint: Are you wearing jeans right now? If so, rub them with your hands and say aloud “Oh, the humanity!” Or just imagine wearing jeans if you aren’t actually wearing them.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Another thing that reminds you and the leader about your <kanji>humanity</kanji> is wearing <reading>jean</reading>s (じん). Jeans are the go-to legwear for many people, and no one but humans wear them. Jeans are another great symbol of humanity.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>leader</radical> has <radical>two</radical> things that remind him of his <kanji>humanity</kanji>: his shoes, and his hat. These two things remind the leader of his humanity because humans are the only ones who wear shoes and hats.

Data.MeaningHint: Think of the wide range of minerals there are that aren't gold. There are a lot.
Data.ReadingHint: Emperor こういち is getting all the minerals, and the 鋼, so he can take over all neighboring nations and force them into a subjugation of efficient kanji learning.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: One person is trying to control all the <kanji>minerals</kanji> of the world. That person is Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Aside from <radical>gold</radical> there are a <radical>wide</radical> variety of other <kanji>mineral</kanji>s as well.

Data.MeaningHint: It's actually pretty easy to attain mole status. Just stand in a field. Wait for the ground to move under you and shove your hand in the earth. Blam! An achievement pops up above your head and you're there.
Data.ReadingHint: If you can attain two animal levels in one day you'll be a legend on the leaderboards.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Mole isn't enough, you want to <kanji>attain</kanji> <reading>shee</reading>p (し) status next. Everyone knows it goes human > mole> sheep. Now you need to stand in the field with the mole and get a sheep to come over.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Dig up a <radical>mole</radical> and you <kanji>attain</kanji> a mole status. You're always trying to attain a new level for yourself and now you get to be on the mole level!

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine saying something with sincerity to someone you want to become important to you. Say something like, “I love you” out loud. Say it with the utmost sincerity.
Data.ReadingHint: Say, "I love you," again and watch your saber beating in time with your heart. If you weren't sincere it would fade and grow dull.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: When you say something with complete, unabashed <kanji>sincerity</kanji> your special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) starts to pulse with the beating of your heart. It shows the person you want to become close to that you truly are sincere!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Something you <radical>say</radical> to someone you <radical>become</radical> attached to should be said with <kanji>sincerity</kanji>. You need to speak with sincerity when you say things to someone who will eventually become your significant other, for example.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture the poop guy from your hometown. Picture him really vividly. The awkward, rude things he does. The way he smells. The way he makes you never want to come back.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine all the wonderful things that exist in your hometown of きょうと. All the things you love to do and see. And now picture poop guy. Ruining it all with his poop hands.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>hometown</kanji> is in <reading>きょう</reading>と which, unfortunately, means your poop guy is in きょうと too. Now every time you go back home he's there stinking everything up.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Everyone has a <radical>poop</radical> <radical>guy</radical> from their <kanji>hometown</kanji>. You know. That poop guy. And every single time you try to visit your hometown, poop guy is there making everyone uncomfortable and reminding you why you left in the first place.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine the little leader sitting on Wolverine’s forehead as he shouts “Invade! Invade invade! Invade his brain and destroy the Wolverine!”
Data.ReadingHint: See the little soldiers slicing open Wolverine’s shin as Wolverine grunts in pain. Getting your leg cut open is not a comfortable thing, even for someone as manly as Wolverine.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Soon they realize that they cannot <kanji>invade</kanji> Wolverine through his head so they travel down to his leg and try to enter through his <reading>shin</reading> (しん). The little soldiers take a big knife and slice open Wolverine’s shin in order to invade his body.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: There is a tiny <radical>leader</radical> on <radical>Wolverine</radical>'s <radical>forehead</radical> sitting on a <radical>stool</radical> and commanding his troops to <kanji>invade</kanji> Wolverine's brain. These tiny soldiers are going to invade Wolverine's body in an attempt to destroy him. The leader himself isn't going to invade Wolverine's brain, he's just going to sit on the stool and supervise.

Data.MeaningHint: Look at this fake leader. See him scramble into a uniform. Does he look like a real leader? Or can you tell that he is a fake?
Data.ReadingHint: Watch as the guillotine comes down. You close your eyes for a second, and when you open them you see that the leader's body was a fake! Wait, where did he go?!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Well, everyone found out he was a <kanji>fake</kanji> anyway, and now you're all going to use a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ) to cut off his fake head.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> gets a nice uniform for the <radical>sake</radical> of not wanting to look like a <kanji>fake</kanji>. The leader is a fake, he just doesn’t want to look like one. He’s not really a leader, but he thinks he should get an official-looking uniform for the sake of not looking like a fake.

Data.MeaningHint: Of course there isn't a tiger in your mouth. And those spikes are just normal teeth. No matter how you look at it, you're saying a lie.
Data.ReadingHint: Now at least you're not lying about spikes being in your mouth.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Lie</kanji> about spikes in your mouth and I'll put soap with spikes in it in your mouth to teach you a lesson. That's why I'm using <reading>u</reading>ni <reading>so</reading>ap (うそ).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Open your <radical>mouth</radical>. A <radical>tiger</radical> fell in there on some <radical>spikes</radical>! That's what you tell everybody, but when they look in your mouth there's nothing there. It's all a big <kanji>lie</kanji>!

Data.MeaningHint: Picture your amazing and terrible ice wife. She's floating in your backyard cackling and shooting ice all around.
Data.ReadingHint: Now you're floating in the backyard cackling with your amazing wife as you use your terrible saber and freeze things with her!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: She turns her <kanji>amazing</kanji> gaze onto your <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) and hits it with an ice beam. Woah! Instead of turning to ice or shattering, it steals her awful powers. Now your saber is amazing and terrible too!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>ice</radical> <radical>wife</radical> is <kanji>amazing</kanji>. She's also <kanji>terrible</kanji>, because she's very cold and has many ice powers, but that's also part of what makes her amazing!

Data.MeaningHint: The townsfolk agree with your mouth, you are noisy and you are picky and you need to shut up.
Data.ReadingHint:
"Ken, your armor color is so last year. That squid you have is gross. Your sword is so gaudy. You talk like you're from the Edo Period, but it's Reiwa right now."

Your noisy and picky self just goes off on Ken the Samurai. He skulks off, defeated by your noisy and picky words.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You are so <kanji>noisy</kanji> and loud that the townsfolk hired <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the Samurai to shut you up.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> <radical>proclaim</radical>s "I am <kanji>noisy</kanji> and I am loud!" It keeps repeating this over and over to everyone you pass by.

Data.MeaningHint: Your mouth is showy because it's covered in grills and bright red lipstick. People get rowdy because you're a celebrity and your showy mouth is your signature look, and everyone's so excited to see your mouth in person.
Data.ReadingHint: As you drive away in your car you flash that signature mouth at them one more time, and you can see them getting even more rowdy.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Everyone is getting <kanji>rowdy</kanji> so you get in your <reading>ca</reading>r (か) to escape.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> is <radical>showy</radical> so it makes everybody around you <kanji>rowdy</kanji>.
